findings.summary: integration starvation — 3+ consecutive named insights with no dwell beat between them (criterion #13 broken band; §7.3 dwell contract)

run_excerpt: The way out of burnout is not more force, but a changed relationship with effort itself.
Here is the turn that catches most people by surprise.
This is not a call to do nothing.

This is why scrolling a phone for two hours leaves a person more depleted, not less.

findings.summary: integration starvation — 3+ consecutive named insights with no dwell beat between them (criterion #13 broken band; §7.3 dwell contract)

run_excerpt: The goal is to act against it once, and to notice that the sky did not fall.
This is the hard part, because the pace is usually not entirely within a person's control.
It is the body accurately reporting the cost of sustained overfunctioning.

This is why some people burn out repeatedly, recovering just enough to return to the same pace and break again.

findings.summary: integration starvation — 3+ consecutive named insights with no dwell beat between them (criterion #13 broken band; §7.3 dwell contract)

run_excerpt: This is why a day of small output can leave you so depleted.
You only have to notice it — to see that the exhaustion is not proof you did too little, and not proof you are failing,
Turns out the peer is also struggling.

findings.summary: integration starvation — 3+ consecutive named insights with no dwell beat between them (criterion #13 broken band; §7.3 dwell contract)

run_excerpt: The exhaustion that lands is not the cost of this week.
It is the cost of years.
This is not a weakness to overcome or a constraint to optimize around — it is a biological and psychological fact.
